title: Chapter 2: viral.exe
content: The phone is having a seizure on my floor.

I mean that literally. It's 6:00 AM and my phone has vibrated itself off the nightstand and onto the hardwood and it's just *going* — bzzbzzbzzbzz — like a small angry animal trying to burrow through the floorboards. I reach down and my fingers are so cold from the apartment — I still haven't paid the heating bill, I still haven't paid *any* bill — that the metal rim of the phone stings when I grab it.
The Mephisto app is open. I don't remember leaving it open.

Numbers are moving across the screen. Not moving. *Exploding.* Followers: 1,247,893. I blink. The comma migrates. Followers: 1,312,047. The digits are spinning like a slot machine that hit jackpot and forgot to stop.

I had forty-seven followers last night. Forty-seven. I know because I checked at 2 AM while I was eating cereal over the sink and hating myself. Forty-seven. One was my mom. One was a bot called @CryptoWolf_88. The remaining forty-five were people who followed me by accident and never bothered to unfollow.

My notifications are a waterfall. I'm not being dramatic — they're sliding down the screen so fast I can't read them. Brands. Magazines. Gallery invitations. DMs from people with blue checkmarks and names I half-recognize from internet famous-adjacent circles. A Victoria's Secret model liked my post. A Victoria's Secret model *liked my post.*

"This is a glitch," I say out loud, because apparently I'm the kind of person who talks to herself now. The apartment doesn't answer. The apartment is too busy being freezing and smelling like the coffee I made at 11 PM and forgot to drink. It's still sitting on the nightstand. I take a sip without thinking. Cold, bitter, stale. Tastes like regret. I drink it anyway.
I close the Mephisto app. I open Instagram.
Okay. *Okay.*

The first thing I see is my own grid and I don't recognize it. My depressed 3 AM art posts — the ones I made during the lowest moments of the lowest year of my life — have been... remastered? Is that the word? The algorithm didn't just boost them. It *reframed* them. The colors that I'd deliberately desaturated because that's how the world looked to me at 3 AM — those colors are back. Brighter than I remember making them. The compositions are tighter, like someone cropped them just so, found the golden ratio hiding in my misery and pulled it into focus.

I scroll. There's a black-and-white photo I took of a cracked coffee cup. The cup from the diner on 5th, the one that came to me already broken, and I photographed it because I thought: *same.* I'd captioned it "everything breaks eventually" because I was in a mood and I almost deleted it five times but never did.
That post has four hundred thousand likes.

The caption is being *quoted.* I find it on a thread with 90K retweets: "the defining statement of post-capitalist alienation." There's a think piece in Vulture. Someone with a PhD tweeted it. Art critics — actual art critics with actual degrees and actual opinions — are calling me "the voice of a generation." I am not the voice of anything. I'm a girl who photographed a broken cup because it was Tuesday and I was sad.

But the numbers don't care about my imposter syndrome. The numbers keep climbing. 1.4 million. 1.5 million. The notification sounds are a continuous tone now — pings layering on top of pings until my phone is just *singing* at me, this mechanical chorus of approval. I should feel something. I should feel incredible. That's what the numbers mean, right? The numbers mean I've made it. The numbers mean I won.
I swipe to my bank app because there's a push notification I almost missed in the chaos.
Direct deposit from Mephisto MCN.
I read it again.

I sit down on the floor. Not gracefully — I just kind of fold, like someone cut my strings. The cold seeps through my pajama pants immediately. I don't care. I'm reading the number and my brain is doing math it hasn't had the luxury to do in years. Student loans: gone. Back rent: gone. I could buy groceries. Real groceries. The kind that come in bags, not the kind you eat over the sink. I could *eat.*
And then I laugh.

It's not a happy laugh. I want to be clear about that. It's the laugh that comes out when reality cracks open and all the logical structures you've built to survive suddenly don't apply anymore. It's a deranged laugh. My neighbor is going to think I've lost it. Maybe I have. I'm sitting on my freezing floor in my freezing apartment and I have forty-seven thousand dollars and 1.5 million followers and last night I signed something I didn't read on an app that appeared from nowhere and I haven't even processed that part yet.
I refresh the bank app.
It's still climbing.

I laugh again. Harder this time. Tears are coming out but I'm not sad, I'm not happy, I'm just — *overwhelmed.* My body doesn't know what emotion to assign to this so it's doing all of them at once. The phone keeps buzzing. The screen keeps glowing. I'm bathed in blue light at 6 AM on a Tuesday and the whole world has decided I matter.
I'm still scrolling — through the comments, the DMs, the breathless articles being banged out in real time — when it happens.
A notification appears.

Not on Instagram. On the Mephisto app. It slides down from the top of my screen with a red border I haven't seen before — this aggressive, corporate red, like the warning color on a hazard sign. The font is different too. Clean. Sans-serif. No emojis, no friendliness, no "hey bestie" energy. Just:
[Thank you for your contribution. Your "empathy" has been deducted.]
I stare at it.
"Empathy has been deducted." I read it three times. The quotation marks around "empathy" are the worst part. Like it's a line item. Like it's a SKU. Like it's something you can just... take.
I swipe it away.

My finger moves before my brain does. Gut reflex. The notification vanishes. I pull down my notification center to find it again and — nothing. It's gone. Not in my history. Not in the app's notification log. Not anywhere. It's like it was never there at all, like I imagined it while sitting on this freezing floor with coffee breath and a life that doesn't belong to me.
"It was a bug," I say.
The apartment doesn't believe me either.

It was a bug. Apps have bugs. This app clearly has a lot of bugs because it just gave me fifty-two thousand dollars and made me famous overnight, which is not exactly standard app behavior. A notification glitch is the least weird thing that's happened in the last six hours.

I get up. My legs are stiff. I pace. The floorboards creak in the familiar places — third board from the kitchen, seventh board from the bathroom. Some things are still normal. The apartment is still cold. The coffee is still stale. I take another sip. Worse now. Room temperature regret.

I should be on cloud nine right now. I *know* this. I've imagined this exact scenario a thousand times — the breakthrough, the viral moment, the door finally opening — and in every fantasy, I'm crying happy tears and calling my mom and spinning around my apartment like Maria in West Side Story. But I'm not doing any of that. I'm standing here, and the joy is...

It's there. It is. I can feel it somewhere. But it's like someone turned the volume down. Like my happiness is a song playing in another room and I can hear the bass but I can't make out the melody. It's at 60%. Maybe 55%. The emotions are *labeled correctly* — I know I should be ecstatic, the architecture of excitement is in place, but the actual *feeling* is behind glass.

Shock. It's got to be shock. I've been broke so long that my happiness muscles are probably atrophied. You don't run a marathon after two years on the couch. You don't feel pure joy after four years of anxiety and ramen. It'll come. The money is real. The followers are real. My body just needs time to catch up to my circumstances.
Right?
I open my camera.

I haven't taken a selfie in months. Not because I don't care about how I look — because I care too much about how I look and the answer was always "not great." But right now I want evidence. Proof that this morning happened. Proof that I was here when everything changed.
The front camera loads and I see my face and — something is off.

Not my skin, which is whatever. Not my hair, which is a disaster. It's my eyes. There's something missing in my eyes and I can't name it. It's not tiredness — I'm used to seeing tiredness. It's not sadness — I know sadness in the mirror like an old roommate. It's something else. A flatness. Like the light behind them is running at lower wattage.
It's the lighting, I decide. It's 6 AM and my apartment has one window and the bulb in the bathroom has been dead for a week. It's the lighting.
I take the photo anyway. I post it. The caption writes itself, which is weird because I always agonize over captions, but this time my fingers just move: "new chapter."

I watch the likes come in. A thousand. Five thousand. Fifty thousand. One hundred thousand in ten minutes. The number climbs and climbs and each increment should feel like a small electric shock of validation but instead it feels like watching a livestream of someone else's life. Someone who looks like me and lives in my apartment and has my dead eyes but isn't, somehow, me.

I sit on the edge of my bed. The phone buzzes and buzzes and buzzes. The screen glows. The numbers rise. Outside, the city is waking up, and somewhere in it, art critics are writing about my cracked coffee cup, and somewhere in it, a bank is holding fifty-two thousand dollars with my name on it, and somewhere in it, an app with a red icon is quietly keeping books.
Your "empathy" has been deducted.
I didn't see that notification. It was a bug. It's gone.
I'll feel happy later. I'm sure I will.
